elements.sent-2411-002: We propose that neutrons may be generated in high-energy, high-flux photon environments via photo-induced reactions on pre-existing baryons.
elements.sent-2411-003: These photo-hadronic interactions are expected to occur in astrophysical jets and surrounding material.
elements.sent-2411-004: Historically, these reactions have been attributed to the production of high-energy cosmic rays and neutrinos.
elements.sent-2411-005: We estimate the photoproduction off of protons in the context of gamma-ray bursts, where it is expected there will be sufficient baryonic material that may be encompassing or entrained in the jet.
elements.sent-2411-006: We show that typical stellar baryonic material, even material completely devoid of neutrons, can become inundated with neutrons in situ via hadronic photoproduction.
elements.sent-2411-007: Consequently, this mechanism provides a means for collapsars and other astrophysical sites containing substantial flux of high-energy photons to be favorable for neutron-capture nucleosynthesis.
elements.sent-2411-008: The formation of the heaviest elements relies on astrophysical environments with a copious amount of neutrons \citep{Burbidge1957 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Neutrons can be found in the cosmos bound in atomic nuclei or in medium under extreme pressure.
elements.sent-2411-010: Free neutrons are rare, owing to a half-life of less than 15 minutes, see \cite{Wietfeldt2011, Serebrov2011 and references therein.}
elements.sent-2411-011: In stellar interiors free neutrons are produced via low energy nuclear reactions, $\ce{^{13 C + \alpha \rightarrow \ce{^{16 O + n$, and $\ce{^{22 Ne + \alpha \rightarrow \ce{^{25 Mg + n$ \citep{Meyer1998, Gallino1998, Fowler2002 .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Neutrons comprise the bulk of neutron stars, owing to the process of neutronization where the Fermi energy of electrons becomes high enough to energetically favor capture with protons via inverse beta decay \citep{Oppenheimer1939, Bethe1995 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: An ample supply of neutrons make the merger of compact objects (neutron star-neutron star or neutron star-black hole) viable candidate sites for the rapid neutron capture ($r$ process) nucleosynthesis \citep{Lattimer1974, Freiburghaus1999, Rosswog2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) may also be promising heavy element factories.
elements.sent-2411-015: As mentioned above, so-called short GRBs, `sGRBs', (GRBs with the prompt emission lasting less that about two seconds) are believed to originate from the merger of two neutron stars \citep{Ab17 or a neutron star-black hole merger; the extremely neutron-rich material present in these merger events is conducive to a robust $r$ process.}
elements.sent-2411-016: In the context of so-called long GRBs, `lGRBs', (GRBs with prompt gamma-ray emission lasting more than about two seconds), the picture is less clear.
elements.sent-2411-017: These GRBs are believed to originate from the collapse of a massive star \citep{Woos93, MW98 which forms an accretion disk engine around a compact object that launches a relativistic jet.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Here we focus on a central black hole, in the so-called collapsar model.\footnote{We note that a so-called magnetar central engine with a magnetized neutron star compact object is also a viable model \citep{Usov92, DT92 , but we consider the black hole-disk central engine in this paper.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Several studies have suggested the presence of $r$ process nucleosynthesis in the disk around the newly born remnant black hole \citep{Fujimoto2007, Siegel19, Anand24 , while others have suggested that the $r$-process is severely suppressed in these systems \citep[e.g.][]{Miller2020, Just22, Blanch24 .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: The presence of conditions suitable for nucleosynthesis in the GRB jet, cocoon (hot area encompassing the jet), and stellar envelope region remains relatively unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-021: Although the baryon density in GRB jets is necessarily very low in order to accelerate these jets to their inferred ultra-relativistic velocities, the baryon density in the stellar material surrounding a lGRB central engine, as well as the cocoon region created as the jet traverses this stellar envelope, have much higher baryon density.
elements.sent-2411-022: Additionally, dense shells ejected near the end of the massive star's life may provide yet another potential site for heavy element production, when the $\gamma$-rays from the jet interact with these regions.
elements.sent-2411-023: The interaction between light and matter in and around these regions can lead to extreme conditions that may be suitable for nucleosynthesis.
elements.sent-2411-024: High-energy, high-flux photon interactions with baryonic matter are relevant for nucleosynthesis in two ways.
elements.sent-2411-025: First, high energy photons have sufficient energy to break apart existing atomic nuclei via the process of photodisentigration.
elements.sent-2411-026: Second, for a range of high-energy photons, hadronic photo-interactions may transmute neutrons to protons or vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-027: In this work, we investigate a neutron production mechanism associated with a high flux of high-energy photons.
elements.sent-2411-028: We explore this possibility in the context of lGRBs associated with collapsars.
elements.sent-2411-029: In Section \ref{sec:picture we provide an overview of the physical picture of lGRBs and how photohadronic processes can create neutrons relevant for nucleosynthesis.}
elements.sent-2411-030: In the remaining sections, we provide the models and details which support this picture.

elements.sent-2411-032: Section \ref{sec:neutrons covers the photoproduction of neutrons.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Section \ref{sec:interactions covers the dynamical interactions between regions of interest to the problem.}
elements.sent-2411-034: We simulate nucleosynthesis based on our model parameters in Section \ref{sec:nucleosynthesis .}
elements.sent-2411-035: In Section \ref{sec:sites we investigate other sites where photo-hadronic processes may emerge and assess their capacity for nucleosynthesis.}
elements.sent-2411-036: We end with a discussion of potential observational signatures and concluding remarks.
elements.sent-2411-037: Gamma-ray burst jets are readily launched in black hole-disk systems (created when a massive star collapses) through the Blandford-Znajek (BZ) process \citep{BZ77, MT82 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Frame-dragging effects around a rapidly rotating black hole wind up magnetic fields (present in the disk, near the black hole horizon) and create a strong Poynting flux along the spin axis of the black hole \citep[for a brief discussion of the BZ process in the context of GRBs see][and references therein]{LR19 .}
elements.sent-2411-039: As this energetic jet is launched, a ``fireball" forms---a radiation-dominated volume of plasma that is optically thick to pair production, typically containing a small amount of baryons.
elements.sent-2411-040: The fireball is accelerated, with Lorentz factor $\Gamma \propto r$, where $r$ is the distance of the jet head from the center of the star/central engine.
elements.sent-2411-041: For $10^{7}$ cm $\lesssim r \lesssim 10^{10}$ cm, the radiation energy in the fireball is converted to kinetic energy of the baryons.
elements.sent-2411-042: In the matter-dominated phase at $r \gtrsim 10^{10}$cm, the jet coasts with approximately constant ultra-relativistic velocity, with $\Gamma \gtrsim 100$.
elements.sent-2411-043: It may still be optically thick to pair production at this point, up until the opacity drops below the pair production threshold in the frame of the jet\footnote{We note that the optical depth to pair production is reduced by a factor of $\sim 1/\Gamma^{6}$ in the frame of the jet \cite{LS01 .}}
elements.sent-2411-044: The flow is still optically thick to electron scattering until about $r \gtrsim 10^{12}$ cm, at which point the photons---particularly the $\gamma$-rays produced in internal dissipation processes such as shocks or magnetic reconnection events---can escape freely from the region.
elements.sent-2411-045: This is the so-called prompt emission phase of a GRB, at $10^{12}$ cm $ \lesssim r \lesssim 10^{15}$ cm, where synchrotron and inverse-Compton processes produce the initial highly variable ``burst'' of $\gamma$-rays, lasting tens of seconds and peaking at about 500 keV (in the observer's frame).
elements.sent-2411-046: Beyond this radius, the ``afterglow'' phase begins: this is the point where the front of the jet has swept up enough of the external medium such that the rest mass energy of the swept up material equals the kinetic energy of the jet, and the jet decelerates.
elements.sent-2411-047: For additional reviews, see \cite{Pir04,ZM04, Mesz06, GRRF09, KZ15,Lev16 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: The jet has to travel through stellar material surrounding the central engine (material from the progenitor star that has not circularized into the disk around the black hole)---we term this stellar material the ``envelope''.
elements.sent-2411-049: As it does so, it forms a cocoon---a hot, relatively dense (compared to the jet) region around the jet with a thermal X-ray spectrum \citep{RR02, NP18, DeColle18, DeColle2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-050: In addition to these interactions in the envelope and cocoon regions, the jet photons can also interact with dense shells of matter ejected before the end of the progenitors lifetime.
elements.sent-2411-051: The regions of interest in a lGRB are shown in Figure \ref{fig:cocoon .}
elements.sent-2411-052: The jet head is the contact interface between the jet and the stellar envelope.
elements.sent-2411-053: It is in this region that existing baryonic material is exposed to a large flux of high-energy photons; the surrounding cocoon is therefore the most likely place for nucleosynthesis to develop.
elements.sent-2411-054: At the interface of light and matter in the jet head region, a large flux of high-energy photons enables exceedingly fast transmutation reactions between protons and neutrons while simultaneously producing mesons.
elements.sent-2411-055: These so-called photo-pion reactions have been traditionally associated with high-energy cosmic rays and neutrinos \citep{Berezinskii1975, Mannheim1995, Waxman1998, Murase2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-056: Relativistic Lorentz contraction results in an effective compression of the stellar density as light interacts with matter; this process creates relatively high density (maximally: $2 \sqrt{2 \Gamma \rho_env$) in the jet head region as compared to the existing stellar density ($\rho_env$).}
elements.sent-2411-057: Here $\Gamma$ is the Lorentz factor of the shock front in the frame of the star.
elements.sent-2411-058: In order for this higher density material to become active for nucleosynthesis, it must escape the jet head into the cocoon.
elements.sent-2411-059: Coincidentally, as the jet head plows through the star, it moves the transformed material out of the way at relativistic speeds.
elements.sent-2411-060: Relatively strong magnetic fields are expected to be generated in the jet environment \citep{Pir05, ZY11, HK13}\footnote{We note the very high magnetic field that launched the jet at the central engine site has decreased significantly at the radii where we suggest neutron production to emerge.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Neutrons on the other hand have no charge, and therefore have a chance to escape; these particles are expected to escape at roughly the speed of the fluid velocity of the material that is being pushed out of the way of the jet.
elements.sent-2411-062: Conservation of baryon number reduces the baryon density in the jet head while the baryon density in the cocoon increases.
elements.sent-2411-063: The jet acts as its own sieve to filter out neutrons into the cocoon.
elements.sent-2411-064: Finally, the escape of a profusion of neutrons (at high density) mixing with relatively lower density material of the stellar envelope enables the cocoon region to become neutron rich.
elements.sent-2411-065: With a plethora of free neutrons, nucleosynthesis will proceed with haste.
elements.sent-2411-066: The efficiency with which baryons escape from the jet head plays a crucial role in determining the initial electron fraction ($Y_e$), which is a key parameter governing nucleosynthesis in the cocoon.
elements.sent-2411-067: Furthermore, the continuous mixing of material within the cocoon affects the temporal evolution of both temperature and density.
elements.sent-2411-068: Variations in temperature and density can alter the synthesis of different isotopes thereby influencing the extent of nucleosynthesis.

elements.section-2411-005: Dynamical interactions between the jet, envelope, and cocoon, and between the jet and previously ejected shells
elements.sent-2411-212: As the jet traverses the stellar envelope, it pushes material out of its way and a hot cocoon forms around the jet with temperatures falling in the $10$'s of keV range \citep{RR02, MLB10, Laz15 .}
elements.sent-2411-213: The interface between the jet and the envelope creates a shock front and region between the jet and envelope.
elements.sent-2411-214: We have been referring to this region as the jet head.
elements.sent-2411-215: The shock interface can be described by the relativistic Rankine-Hugonoit jump conditions \citep{Taub1948 .}
elements.sent-2411-216: In the frame of the star, the post-shock jet head baryonic density is Lorentz contracted.
elements.sent-2411-217: For an ultra-relativistic shock, the maximum value it can reach is \begin{equation}\label{eqn:rh_rho_max} \rho^max_head = 2 \sqrt{2} \Gamma \rho_env\end{equation} where $\Gamma$ is the Lorentz factor of the jet (shock) and $\rho_env$ is the baryonic density of the (pre-shock) envelope \citep{Blandford1976 .}
elements.sent-2411-218: In actuality, there will be some efficiency in which relativistic hydrodynamical processes increase the density of the jet head.
elements.sent-2411-219: We parameterize this efficiency via $\epsilon$, which ranges between 1 and $2 \sqrt{2 \Gamma$, \begin{equation}\label{eqn:rh_rho_eps} \rho_head = \epsilon \rho_env \ .\end{equation}.}

elements.subsection-2411-017: Setting the initial electron fraction for nucleosynthesis
elements.sent-2411-245: The electron fraction of the material is difficult to ascertain due to its dependence on the dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-246: In the most extreme case, all baryons in the jet head are converted to neutrons, escape, and this neutron gas in turn mixes with material in the cocoon.
elements.sent-2411-247: The maximum ratio of the jet head number density (all neutrons) to the pre-existing stellar envelope density (all protons) is \begin{equation}\label{eqn:yearg} \frac{n_head}{n_env} = \frac{2 \sqrt{2} \Gamma \rho_env m_p}{\rho_env m_n} = 2 \sqrt{2} \Gamma \frac{m_p}{m_n} = \frac{Y_n}{Y_p} .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-248: Considering only a population of neutrons and protons, the electron fraction is \begin{equation}\label{eqn:yeratio} Y_e = \frac{Y_p}{Y_n + Y_p} = \frac{1}{Y_n/Y_p + 1} \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-249: The minimum electron fraction for a dense neutron gas streaming into a less dense gas of protons is then a function of $\Gamma$, \begin{equation}\label{eqn:yenandp} Y^min_e = \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2} \frac{m_p}{m_n} \Gamma + 1} \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-250: This value becomes the initial electron fraction of the cocoon.

elements.sent-2411-254: If achieved in nature, these excessively low electron fractions provide a natural explanation for the universality of the $r$-process pattern above $Z\gtrsim 50$ \citep{Cowan1999, Sakari2018, Farouqi2022 .}

elements.sent-2411-256: The density of the escaped neutrons may also be reduced via dynamical processes.

elements.sent-2411-258: Using Eq.~\ref{eqn:rh_rho_eps and starting from free protons, the initial electron fraction for nucleosynthesis under the assumption of perfect mixing and full escape of all neutrons is then \begin{eqnarray}\label{eqn:Ye0} Y_e = \frac{1}{\epsilon \frac{m_p}{m_n} + 1} \ .\end{eqnarray}.}

elements.sent-2411-264: As the first gas moves into the second, it picks up mass, \begin{equation}\label{eqn:dmdx} \frac{dm_1}{dx} = \rho_2 S \ ,\end{equation} where $m_1$ is the mass of the first gas, $\rho_2$ is the density of the second gas and $S$ is the cross sectional area traversed over distance $dx$.
elements.sent-2411-265: As a function of distance, the mass of the first gas is \begin{equation}\label{eqn:mx} m_1(x) = m_{1} + \rho_2 S x \ ,\end{equation} where $m_{1}$ is the initial mass of gas 1.

elements.sent-2411-267: The mixing length is \begin{equation}\label{eqn:mixdist} L = \frac{m_1}{\rho_2 S} \ .\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-421: A complete picture of the spatial, energetic, and temporal statistics of particles requires use of the Boltzmann transport equations.
elements.sent-2411-422: These coupled integro-differential equations describe the evolution of the particle number as a function of phase space (position and momentum) and time.
elements.sent-2411-423: Because Boltzmann transport is extremely difficult to solve in practice, simplifying assumptions are made to make the problem more tractable.
elements.sent-2411-424: For instance, to follow the population of relativistic particles one can use the kinetic equation formulated by \cite{Ginzburg1964 .}
elements.sent-2411-425: Assuming the distribution of particles is homogeneous in space (uniformly mixed) removes the spatial gradients in the Boltzmann description.
elements.sent-2411-426: With this simplifying assumption, one can imagine tracking the interactions in a small parcel of material that co-moves with a fluid element in the environment.
elements.sent-2411-427: The tracking of species without reference to spatial dependencies is often referred to as a `single zone' reaction network.
elements.sent-2411-428: Any interactions that inject or remove particles from this system can be treated as source or loss terms respectively in the simplified differential equation(s); this is a loss of information compared to the solution of Boltzmann transport.
elements.sent-2411-429: Contrastingly, multi-zone networks keep track of the movement of particles between neighboring fluid elements, see e.g. work by the NuGrid collaboration \citep{Herwig2008,Battino2019 , giving insight into spatial dependencies.}
elements.sent-2411-430: A second simplifying assumption can be made regarding the energy distribution of the participating species.
elements.sent-2411-431: When particles follow relatively fixed energy distributions, each distribution may be integrated over to obtain the composition of the specified species as a function of a single independent variable, time.
elements.sent-2411-432: Reaction networks used in astrophysics that track elemental compositions make this assumption, typically with the distribution of species assumed to be Maxwell-Boltzmann like, see e.g.~\citep{Lippuner2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-433: In what follows, we take both simplifying assumptions.
